VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides enterprise-wide continuous monitoring through integrated tools that span infrastructure health, performance metrics, and log analysis.

VCF Operations continuously monitors the operational state of all VCF components, collecting metrics and correlating them over time to identify trends. Administrators define alert policies based on performance metrics, log events, or custom conditions, with log forwarding to external SIEM or syslog targets configurable with TLS encryption.

VCF Operations for Logs centralizes log collection and analysis from vCenter, ESX hosts, and other VCF components, providing dashboards, analytics, and log-based alerts for specific patterns.

The vSphere layer includes an alarm infrastructure for real-time alerting based on events and performance counters, with configurable monitoring intervals.

VCF Health continuously monitors component health, offering a unified view for operators to configure alerts, perform log analysis, and review status.

VCF Automation integration provides real-time capacity and alert information for VCF Automation objects, and VCF Operations supports self-monitoring of its own platform.

VCF also provides technical instrumentation for cybersecurity incidents, including a Troubleshooting Incidents page and alert-driven incident detection with configurable impact levels, integrating with ServiceNow for incident creation.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) provides continuous monitoring for disaster recovery operations, including event logging and configurable alarms for replication events, RPO violations, and storage capacity.

VLR monitors replication traffic connectivity between sites, generating alerts for connectivity loss and resuming monitoring automatically upon restoration. The vSphere Replication management interface offers a consolidated view of VM replication and connectivity states.

VLR health and replication status data integrate into VCF Operations via a management pack, contributing to enterprise-wide operational visibility and issue tracking.

VMware vDefend maintains continuous visibility into network traffic flows across protected workloads through Security Intelligence.

It detects new, unapproved, or blocked flows, alerting administrators to unauthorized or unexpected traffic and continuously monitoring microsegmentation policy compliance. Security Intelligence detects and categorizes suspicious traffic events by severity, mapping them to MITRE ATT&CK tactics and techniques.

The Discover & Take Action view visualizes entities and traffic flows, while the blast radius view identifies lateral movement patterns and assesses incident scope.

vDefend tracks detected versus prevented events, summarizes suspicious events, and maintains a 30-day record of IDS/IPS events.

Network Detection and Response (NDR) correlates individual detection events into campaigns, providing a higher-level view of threat activity.

vDefend Advanced Threat Prevention logs IDS/IPS alerts, and the Security Services Platform raises alarms categorized by severity.

These platforms collectively provide the comprehensive technical capabilities to continuously monitor for a wide range of security-relevant events across the infrastructure, network, and disaster recovery domains.

However, the customer is responsible for defining specific security policies, configuring the precise thresholds and log patterns that constitute "security-relevant events" for their environment, and establishing the organizational processes for incident notification, coordination of response teams, and tracking incident resolution through to closure.

The operationalization of incident management workflows and communication responsibilities requires customer-defined procedures and potentially dedicated ITSM or SOAR tooling.

Description:
Denial of service protection: Capability to maintain essential functions during a DoS attack.

Resource management: Manage resources to prevent exhaustion.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, VMware Live Recovery, and VMware vDefend collectively provide extensive capabilities to maintain essential functions and manage resources.

The platform ensures resilience through vSphere High Availability, Fault Tolerance, Proactive HA, and vMotion for compute, alongside multi-NIC teaming, LACP, and NSX gateway high availability for network continuity.

Storage resilience is delivered via vSAN fault domains, stretched clusters, and multipathing.

VMware Live Site Recovery further extends this by automating site-level failover for critical workloads, including replication, IP customization, and test failover capabilities, ensuring rapid recovery during major disruptions.

Resource management is robustly addressed through vSphere Distributed Resource Scheduler (DRS) for automated load balancing, resource pools with reservations and limits, Network I/O Control (NIOC) for bandwidth prioritization, and vSAN IOPS limits to prevent storage exhaustion.

VMware vDefend enhances these capabilities with Distributed Firewall flood protection for ICMP, UDP, and TCP SYN attacks, along with IDS/IPS for signature-based and behavioral detection and blocking of DoS patterns.

Malicious IP filtering, NSX QoS profiles, broadcast rate limiting, and SpoofGuard further contribute to mitigating network-based denial of service conditions and preventing resource exhaustion within the software-defined data center.

While the platform offers comprehensive internal DoS protection and resource management, it does not provide dedicated perimeter-level volumetric DDoS mitigation.

Organizations must implement external scrubbing services or ISP-level solutions to protect against large-scale attacks that saturate upstream network links before traffic reaches the virtualized environment.

Additionally, customers are responsible for defining and implementing security policies, configuring appropriate resource limits and QoS profiles, establishing recovery plans, and regularly testing failover procedures to ensure the effectiveness of these capabilities in their specific operational context.

Description:
Denial of service protection: Capability to maintain essential functions during a DoS attack.

Resource management: Manage resources to prevent exhaustion.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms to manage resources and mitigate internal denial of service conditions.

vSphere Distributed Resource Scheduler (DRS) automatically balances compute resources across hosts, while Resource Pools enable hierarchical allocation with reservations, limits, and shares to prevent resource exhaustion. Network I/O Control (NIOC) prioritizes bandwidth for critical traffic types on distributed switches, and vSAN storage policies enforce IOPS limits per object.

At the network layer, NSX Gateway and Segment QoS profiles define traffic rate limits, dropping non-conforming traffic, and broadcast rate limiting protects against broadcast storms. SpoofGuard provides anti-spoofing by validating MAC and IP addresses, and VCF Automation includes an internal host denylist to protect the management network. VCF Operations implements flow limit protection for TCP/UDP and API rate limiting to prevent overload.

VMware vDefend extends these capabilities with advanced denial of service protection within the virtualized network.

The vDefend Distributed Firewall includes built-in flood protection, imposing active session limits for ICMP, UDP, and half-open TCP flows to specifically counter SYN flood attacks by dropping new connections when thresholds are exceeded. Its IDS/IPS engine provides signature-based and behavioral detection of DoS attack patterns, supporting configurable thresholds and rate filters that can trigger automatic blocking in IPS mode.

Malicious IP Filtering automatically blocks traffic from known malicious sources, and the vDefend Gateway Firewall can block traffic based on application-level information, including URL categories and reputation scores, providing granular control to limit protocols vulnerable to application-layer DoS attacks.

While VCF and vDefend offer extensive technical controls, the customer is responsible for defining and configuring specific QoS profiles, resource pool parameters, NIOC policies, vSAN IOPS limits, and vDefend flood protection thresholds, IDS/IPS rules, and firewall policies to align with their organizational requirements and risk posture.

Establishing operational procedures for monitoring, alerting, and incident response is also a customer responsibility.

It is important to note that dedicated perimeter DDoS mitigation services, such as volumetric attack scrubbing, are outside the scope of VCF and vDefend and are required to protect against attacks that saturate upstream network links before traffic reaches the virtualized environment.

Description: Resource management: Allocate resources to prioritize essential functions.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation Operations provides a comprehensive capacity management lifecycle encompassing assessment, optimization, and planning.

This includes computing capacity analytics, tracking resource availability through metrics like `capacityRemainingPercentage`, and presenting detailed dashboards that display resource utilization, allocation, trends, and forecasts across compute, storage, and networking. The platform facilitates efficient resource management through workload optimization based on policy settings and capacity optimization for forecasting the impact of new workloads. Infrastructure planning leverages historical data and predictive projections to forecast future capacity needs, with configurable capacity policy settings, such as CPU utilization thresholds, enabling proactive resource allocation.

VMware vSAN further extends these capabilities by providing storage-specific capacity and performance metrics for integrated planning.

VMware vDefend includes dedicated capacity and performance management capabilities for its security processing infrastructure via the Security Services Platform.

This platform offers a Host Capacity view, providing visibility into system resource utilization, flow metrics, and prioritized applications and groups, which aids in determining resource allocation for security functions. Built-in capacity thresholds and planning guidance recommend actions like adding worker nodes or rebalancing VMs when capacity approaches limits. Resource reservation is enabled by default for the Security Services Platform, ensuring essential security components have guaranteed CPU and memory. Host Transport Node Metrics monitoring helps detect capacity constraints within the Distributed Firewall processing layer, allowing for optimized resource allocation to prevent degradation of security enforcement.

While vDefend manages capacity within its security domain, broader infrastructure capacity planning for compute, storage, and networking is handled by VCF Operations.

While the platform provides robust technical capabilities for resource management, monitoring, planning, and the mechanisms to prioritize essential functions, the customer is responsible for defining what constitutes an "essential function" within their specific operational context. This includes establishing the business-driven policies and configurations that dictate how resources are prioritized and allocated to meet the performance and availability requirements of those essential functions.

Description:
Control system backup: Support the backup of configuration and user data.

Verify backup integrity: Capability to verify the integrity of backups.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms for backing up configuration and user data across its management and workload layers. SDDC Manager, NSX Manager, and VMware vCenter support file-based backups, with SDDC Manager also offering image-based options.

Workload-level data protection is enabled through Changed Block Tracking for incremental backups and VMware vStorage APIs for Data Protection, facilitating integration with third-party backup solutions.

VMware Kubernetes Service supports backup and restore for cluster management workloads, including CSI volume snapshots.

vSAN Data Protection allows for external replication of snapshots, enhancing data resilience.

VMware Live Recovery provides continuous host-based replication and point-in-time snapshots for protected virtual machines, with recovery plan testing serving as a direct mechanism to verify data usability and integrity at the recovery site.

VMware vDefend secures its own control plane, including firewall rules, microsegmentation policies, and IDS/IPS configurations, through scheduled, encrypted backups to an SFTP server, maintaining a backup history for status confirmation.

While the platform delivers the necessary technical capabilities, customers are responsible for configuring backup schedules, defining retention policies, provisioning sufficient storage capacity, and performing regular restore testing to validate backup integrity and meet defined Recovery Time Objectives and Recovery Point Objectives.

For vDefend, customers must provide and manage the SFTP server, ensure platform health prior to backup operations, and manage certificate consistency during recovery. The underlying VCF platform provides the foundational backup and replication services, but the operationalization and policy enforcement remain customer responsibilities.

Description:
Control system backup: Support the backup of configuration and user data.

Verify backup integrity: Capability to verify the integrity of backups.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive mechanisms for backing up configuration and user data across its management and workload layers. NSX backup monitoring tracks backup configuration updates, successes, and failures, offering visibility into process reliability. Scheduled and on-demand backups can be configured for management components such as SDDC Manager, vCenter, NSX, and VCF Operations. For workload-level data protection, Changed Block Tracking (CBT) enables incremental backups, vSAN Data Protection allows external replication with automated policies, and vSAN Express Storage Architecture (ESA) delivers metadata-based snapshots. VMware vStorage APIs for Data Protection (VADP) offer a standardized interface for third-party backup solutions, which typically include their own verification capabilities. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) further enhances data protection by providing non-disruptive testing mechanisms to verify the reliability of the replication process and the integrity and availability of replicated data at the recovery site. Recovery plan testing uses temporary copies of replicated data to exercise the full recovery sequence without disrupting operations, validating that all virtual machines can be recovered and data loss avoided. vSphere Replication offers Enhanced Replication Mappings testing and quiescing for higher data consistency. The VCF Operations Management Pack for vSphere Replication tracks virtual machine error states, providing monitoring visibility into replication health. VMware vDefend Security Services Platform (SSP) contributes by tracking backup history and monitoring successful completion of scheduled operations, ensuring accurate time synchronization and certificate consistency, and using Machine Health Check (MHC) for node replacement, which supports the reliability of the platform state being backed up.

While the platform provides the technical capabilities for backup and integrity verification, the customer is responsible for defining and implementing organizational procedures for routine testing of backup reliability and active data integrity validation. This includes configuring backup jobs, establishing schedules, performing actual restore operations for management components and workload-level snapshots to validate backup data integrity, and regularly testing recovery plans. For vDefend, active backup restoration testing and integrity validation of backup content require additional organizational testing procedures outside the automated scope of the platform.

Description: Control system recovery and reconstitution: Capability to recover to a known secure state.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive capabilities for the secure recovery and reconstitution of infrastructure and workloads to a known state following disruption or failure. vSphere HA, Fault Tolerance, and Proactive HA ensure compute workload resilience and automated restarts. vSAN stretched clusters and fault domains deliver site-level recovery and data resilience, complemented by vSAN Data Protection for snapshot-based point-in-time recovery. Platform management components like vCenter and VCF Operations support file-based and image-based restore operations, including reconciliation APIs to restore topology and infrastructure state. VMware Live Recovery orchestrates the secure recovery of virtual machine workloads through protection groups and recovery plans, supporting both planned migration and disaster recovery with multiple replication methods, non-disruptive testing, and secure TLS communications. VMware vDefend further contributes by providing backup and restore functionality for its own security infrastructure, ensuring the recovery of Security Intelligence, Network Detection and Response, and Malware Prevention Service configurations. Crucially, NSX Federation maintains established security frameworks, including Distributed Firewall rules and microsegmentation policies, across sites during failover events, ensuring the security posture follows workloads to the recovery site and preserving the "secure" aspect of the recovered state.

While the platform provides the technical mechanisms for recovery, the customer is responsible for defining the specific "known secure state" for their environment, including RPOs and RTOs. This includes configuring and maintaining protection groups, recovery plans, and backup schedules for all relevant components. Regular, non-disruptive testing of recovery plans is essential to validate the recovery process and ensure the recovery site is correctly configured. Customers must also establish and document operational procedures for executing and validating recovery operations, including personnel assignments and the maintenance of consistent FQDNs and certificates for security platform backups.

Description: Emergency power: Capability to operate during power interruptions (if applicable).
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Network and security configuration settings: Protect configuration settings from unauthorized changes.

Automated configuration monitoring: Monitor for unauthorized configuration changes.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms to protect network and security configuration settings from unauthorized changes and to monitor for such deviations.

VCF Operations includes Configuration Templates that define a desired state, integrating with source control for versioning, auditing, and change control, which supports reviews and automated testing before changes are applied. Configuration Drifts Templates track deviations from this defined state, enabling detection, review, and remediation of unauthorized changes.

vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) and Configuration Profiles enforce approved host images and configurations, detecting and remediating drift for ESX hosts.

VCF Automation extends these capabilities through integrations with tools like Ansible and Puppet, enabling automated configuration enforcement and monitoring for workloads.

The VCF Security Baseline, component hardening guides, and DISA STIGs provide documented secure configurations, which VCF Operations' compliance scanning capabilities evaluate against, identifying non-compliant objects and offering remediation.

NSX maintains centralized configuration state for virtual networking, with Transport Node Profiles ensuring consistency and vLCM detecting and remediating drift.

VMware Live Recovery contributes by providing structured configuration management for disaster recovery operations. Its Configuration Import/Export Tool supports versioning and backup of DR configuration data, while the hms-configtool and DR IP Customizer facilitate controlled, auditable changes to specific DR settings.

VMware vDefend significantly enhances protection and monitoring within the network security domain. The Distributed Firewall's draft configuration management, with optional locking and comment requirements, ensures policy changes are reviewed and validated before publication, preventing unauthorized modifications.

vDefend's federation architecture provides centralized configuration management for consistent security policy baselines across sites. The Security Services Platform includes deployment validation to verify configuration correctness before implementation and offers Config Sync metrics for visibility into change frequency. Backup and restore capabilities further protect configuration integrity, and Security Assessment evaluates security posture against expected standards.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical tools for configuration protection and monitoring, the organization remains responsible for establishing and enforcing the overarching policies that define authorized changes, access controls, and incident response procedures for detected deviations. This includes integrating VCF's capabilities into a broader organizational configuration management program, managing source control repositories, defining review workflows, and ensuring human accountability for configuration management and remediation.

Furthermore, VCF's configuration synchronization capabilities primarily apply to the virtual networking layer; physical network device configuration synchronization requires external network management tools.

Description:
Network and security configuration settings: Protect configuration settings from unauthorized changes.

Automated configuration monitoring: Monitor for unauthorized configuration changes.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive, automated governance over network and security configuration settings. The Configuration Drifts feature continuously monitors vCenter instances and vSphere clusters against defined desired-state configuration templates, automatically detecting and notifying administrators of any deviations or unauthorized changes.

These templates can be integrated with source control repositories, enabling robust change control workflows with versioned, auditable management of configuration baselines, including reviews and automated validation.

VCF also incorporates built-in compliance benchmarks, such as the VCF Security Baseline, Security Configuration Guide, and DISA STIGs, which evaluate both default and non-default settings across infrastructure components, reporting compliance status and offering actionable remediation recommendations.

Furthermore, vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) enforces desired software state images for ESX clusters through continuous compliance checks, while VCF Operations allows for the configuration of alerts based on configuration property changes, complete with associated remediation guidance.

VMware vDefend further contributes to automated baseline configuration management through Security Intelligence's automated infrastructure classification and the Security Services Platform's validation of system-generated groups, auto-tagging, and Distributed Firewall rules.

In federated environments, a centralized Global Manager streamlines IDS/IPS configuration and security policy management across multiple data centers.

While these capabilities provide automated discovery and policy baseline generation, organizations are responsible for defining the specific desired-state configuration templates, establishing formal change control processes around template modifications, and actively reviewing and responding to drift notifications and compliance violations to ensure continuous adherence to security policies.

Description: Least functionality: Disable unnecessary ports, protocols, and services.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) and VMware vDefend provide comprehensive technical capabilities to enforce least functionality by disabling unnecessary ports, protocols, and services.

VCF establishes a secure foundation through ESX host firewalls, which control service access and support allowed host specifications. Services like SSH can be individually managed, and ESX lockdown mode restricts host access to vCenter, with configurable exceptions. Unexposed features can be disabled via isolation parameters.

At the network layer, VCF leverages distributed switches with port group security policies and VLAN IDs for traffic segmentation. VCF Operations collects ESX security properties, including service status and firewall rules, providing visibility into the least-functionality posture when the vSphere Hardening Guide policy is applied. Management traffic is isolated on dedicated networks, and vSAN automatically configures required firewall ports, ensuring alignment with actual service needs. NSX allows for precise service definition with port and protocol pairings, which are then referenced in firewall rules.

VMware vDefend extends this enforcement with its Distributed Firewall, operating at the vNIC level to enforce granular rules that specify allowed, dropped, or rejected ports, protocols, and services, supporting Layer 2 through Layer 7 filtering. The Security Services Platform (SSP) provides rule optimization analysis, actively identifying overly permissive, duplicate, or ineffective firewall rules, directly supporting least functionality by flagging configurations that allow more traffic than necessary. SSP and Security Intelligence offer traffic flow analysis with port and protocol filtering, enabling administrators to verify that only essential communications occur and to detect unexpected patterns.

Segmentation planning capabilities within SSP allow for defining broad isolation policies between environments. The Gateway Firewall further restricts north-south traffic at logical network segment perimeters, creating a layered enforcement model.

While the platform provides the full technical stack, including identification and enforcement mechanisms, the customer is responsible for defining and implementing specific firewall rules, configuring services, and actively responding to the platform's recommendations and analysis to maintain the desired least-functionality posture.

Description: Component inventory: Capability to provide a list of hardware and software components.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a robust capability to maintain a detailed, real-time inventory of technology assets, encompassing hardware and software components across compute, storage, and networking layers. vCenter Server serves as the authoritative inventory for managed objects, including ESX hosts, clusters, virtual machines, datastores, and networking constructs, with programmatic access available via the vSphere Web Services API.

VCF Operations retrieves and logically groups these inventory details, while its Service Discovery adapter identifies running applications and services, along with their resource metrics, allowing for grouping, export, and visualization through interactive dashboards. VCF Automation tracks provisioned resources such as virtual machines, catalogs, and templates, with inventory views scoped by project roles.

For software components, vSphere Lifecycle Manager maintains a software depot with metadata on installed components and available updates, and VCF Lifecycle Management tracks software versions for compatibility.

Integration with external asset registries is supported through the Management Pack for ServiceNow, which populates the ServiceNow CMDB, and VCF Automation facilitates CMDB reconciliation. VMware vDefend further contributes by maintaining an inventory of network-visible workloads, organized into asset collections for groups, infrastructure, environments, applications, and tiers, supporting import and filtering for reporting.

While the platform offers extensive technical capabilities for component inventory, enterprise asset classification, business justification tracking, and property accountability processes remain organizational responsibilities. The platform's inventory, particularly vDefend's scope, is limited to network-visible workloads and does not replace enterprise-wide inventory tools, authorized software catalogs with business justification records, or data asset registries required for comprehensive asset management.

Description:
Mobile code integrity: Verify the integrity of mobile code before execution.

Mobile code authenticity: Verify the authenticity of mobile code before execution.

Mobile code authorization: Allow only authorized mobile code to execute.

Mobile code security: Provide the capability to enforce a security policy for mobile code.

Mobile code execution environment: Run mobile code in a confined environment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a robust hardware root of trust through integration with Secure Boot, Trusted Platform Module (TPM) 2.0, and Intel Trusted Execution Technology (TXT). This foundation enables verification of mobile code integrity and authenticity before execution by validating boot-time measurements against expected values and reviewing TPM event logs via vCenter APIs. Only authorized code is permitted to execute, enforced by these hardware-level trust mechanisms and further supported by role-based access controls within vSphere Lifecycle Manager for firmware updates.

The platform provides the inherent capability to enforce comprehensive security policies for mobile code. Workloads are run in a highly confined environment provided by the hypervisor, which is further enhanced by confidential computing technologies such as AMD Secure Encrypted Virtualization (SEV-ES, SEV-SNP) and Intel Trust Domain Extensions (TDX). These technologies prevent CPU register state leakage and ensure isolation from the hypervisor, maintaining the integrity and confidentiality of code during execution.

VCF Operations continuously monitors hardware properties, including BIOS versions, to ensure the underlying platform remains current and secure.

Description:
Mobile code integrity: Verify the integrity of mobile code before execution.

Mobile code authenticity: Verify the authenticity of mobile code before execution.

Mobile code authorization: Allow only authorized mobile code to execute.

Mobile code security: Provide the capability to enforce a security policy for mobile code.

Mobile code execution environment: Run mobile code in a confined environment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a robust hardware root of trust for its ESX hosts through the integration of Secure Boot, Trusted Platform Module (TPM) 2.0, and Intel Trusted Execution Technology (TXT). This foundational layer ensures the integrity and authenticity of the platform's boot code and firmware are verified before execution.

vCenter exposes TPM management APIs, allowing administrators to retrieve endorsement keys, review event logs, and validate boot-time measurements against expected values, supporting comprehensive firmware trust configuration with attestation services.

For workload-level protection, VCF supports confidential computing technologies, including AMD Secure Encrypted Virtualization (SEV-ES, SEV-SNP) and Intel Trust Domain Extensions (TDX), which enable workloads to run in a confined, encrypted environment, preventing CPU register state from leaking information to the hypervisor or other components.

Furthermore, vSphere Lifecycle Manager, with its role-based access controls, manages firmware updates, ensuring the platform's embedded device firmware remains current and authorized, while the VCF Security Configuration Guide provides a validated security baseline for hardening platform configurations.

While VMware Cloud Foundation provides a secure execution environment and robust capabilities for verifying the integrity and authenticity of platform code and hosted workloads, the customer retains responsibility for defining and implementing specific security policies for mobile code within their guest operating systems and applications. This includes establishing authorization policies for application-level mobile code, configuring and managing attestation services, and leveraging the provided confidential computing features to meet their specific security requirements for code executing within the virtualized environment.

Description:
Mobile code integrity: Verify the integrity of mobile code before execution.

Mobile code authenticity: Verify the authenticity of mobile code before execution.

Mobile code authorization: Allow only authorized mobile code to execute.

Mobile code security: Provide the capability to enforce a security policy for mobile code.

Mobile code execution environment: Run mobile code in a confined environment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a robust hardware root of trust for each ESXi host through integration with Secure Boot, Trusted Platform Module (TPM) 2.0, and Intel Trusted Execution Technology (TXT). vCenter exposes TPM management APIs, enabling administrators to retrieve endorsement keys, review event logs, and validate boot-time measurements against expected values, thereby verifying the integrity and authenticity of the platform before code execution. For workload-level protection, VCF supports confidential computing technologies, including AMD Secure Encrypted Virtualization (SEV-ES, SEV-SNP) and Intel Trust Domain Extensions (TDX), which create highly confined and secure execution environments for confidential vSphere Pods by preventing CPU register state from leaking information to the hypervisor or other components.

VCF Operations continuously monitors hardware properties, such as BIOS version and release date, and flags hosts running outdated server BIOS. vSphere Lifecycle Manager, with its role-based access controls, ensures authorized and current firmware updates, maintaining the integrity of the platform's embedded code. The VCF Security Configuration Guide provides a validated security baseline, offering the capability to enforce a comprehensive security policy across the platform.

While VMware Cloud Foundation provides a secure and confined execution environment with robust integrity and authenticity verification for the underlying platform and workloads, the customer is responsible for defining and implementing specific security policies for the application-level mobile code they deploy, including its integrity, authenticity, and authorization within the virtualized environment, and for configuring the platform according to the VCF Security Configuration Guide.

Description:
Physical tamper resistance and detection: Capability to detect physical tampering of the embedded device.

Tamper response: Take defined actions (e.g., zeroize keys) upon tamper detection.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms for detecting unauthorized changes to the underlying host firmware through Secure Boot and TPM 2.0 integration. These capabilities enable the platform to identify alterations to boot firmware that could result from physical interface tampering. TPM event logs can be retrieved to validate boot-time measurements, offering a verifiable mechanism to confirm the integrity of host firmware.

Furthermore, VCF establishes stringent controls over which physical devices are exposed to virtual machines, requiring explicit administrator action for assignments such as PCI passthrough for USB, storage, optical disc drives, virtual GPU, and other PCI devices.

Virtual machine properties like `isolation.device.edit.disable` and `isolation.device.connectable.disable` prevent unauthorized modification, removal, or connection of devices at the virtual machine level, thereby contributing to the protection of embedded devices from unauthorized virtual interface access.

While VCF offers critical detection capabilities for firmware integrity and controls virtual access to physical devices, the comprehensive physical protection of server ports, console interfaces, and factory diagnostic connections remains outside the platform's scope.

Organizations are responsible for implementing appropriate physical security measures and leveraging hardware vendor controls to ensure the physical tamper resistance of the server hardware.

Additionally, the definition and implementation of specific tamper response actions, such as key zeroization, upon detection of tampering are the responsibility of the customer.

Description:
Physical tamper resistance and detection: Capability to detect physical tampering of the embedded device.

Tamper response: Take defined actions (e.g., zeroize keys) upon tamper detection.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for detecting unauthorized logical modifications and configuration drift within its embedded infrastructure components, including vCenter and vSphere clusters. This is achieved through continuous configuration monitoring, comparing current settings against desired states, and generating detailed log entries for security-relevant events, configuration changes, and administrative actions across kernel subsystems and services. VCF Operations for Logs aggregates these events, enabling proactive alerting for detected anomalies. VCF also provides logging and alerting for diagnostic and management interface access across its components, with all access subject to role-based controls and audit logging.

While VCF offers extensive capabilities for detecting logical configuration changes and monitoring access to its management and diagnostic interfaces, its scope is focused on the software-defined components and management plane. VCF does not directly manage firmware-level or microcontroller-level logging on server hardware, which is essential for detecting physical tampering of underlying embedded devices. Therefore, customers are responsible for implementing and monitoring physical tamper detection mechanisms provided by their hardware vendors and defining appropriate responses, such as key zeroization, upon physical tamper detection.

Description: Protection from malicious code: Apply malware protection specifically to embedded resources.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) implements comprehensive measures to prevent unauthorized software installation and execution across its infrastructure components and hosted workloads.

The ESX hypervisor enforces code signing, blocking unsigned or tampered binaries from executing. This is further strengthened by Secure Boot, which validates each stage of the boot process against known-good signatures, and Trusted Platform Module (TPM) 2.0 integration, which records boot measurements to detect any unauthorized changes to the boot chain.

Within the virtualization layer, VCF provides controls to restrict device-level interactions that could introduce unauthorized software, utilizing VM properties such as `isolation.device.edit.disable` and `isolation.device.connectable.disable` to prevent unauthorized modification, removal, or connection of devices. Access to physical devices can be revoked by removing the associated virtual device from a VM's configuration, and access attempts from guest operating systems to virtual devices are trapped and handled in software rather than passed directly to hardware.

For containerized workloads, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) supports mutation policies that can alter pod specifications during admission to enforce conformance, including restricting container image sources or blocking privileged containers, with Role-based access control limiting mutation policy management to authorized administrators.

VCF Operations also detects non-compliant configurations and triggers automated remediation actions to maintain compliance.

While these controls effectively protect VCF's own infrastructure and hosted workloads, they do not extend to other embedded devices such as Internet of Things (IoT) or Operational Technology (OT) equipment present in the broader customer environment. The customer is responsible for implementing and managing malware protection solutions for these external embedded resources.

Description:
Support for updates: Embedded device capability to support secure software/firmware updates.

Update authenticity: Verify authenticity of updates via digital signatures.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for securely updating software and firmware on its infrastructure components. vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) implements image-based lifecycle management for ESX hosts and clusters, ensuring a consistent, desired state for ESX versions, compatible partner software, and firmware. For firmware, vLCM integrates with OEM Hardware Support Managers (HSMs) to include vendor-specific updates within the desired-state image, with vSAN clusters specifically requiring firmware updates through vLCM images. SDDC Manager further coordinates lifecycle operations across the VCF stack, managing upgrade and patch bundles through Binary Management, supporting online and offline software depots, and performing prechecks before upgrades. Access to these update operations is strictly controlled through specific VcIntegrity privileges.

VMware vDefend extends this capability to its own distributed security components, including Service Virtual Machines, Security Intelligence appliances, NDR sensors, and bare metal agents. The Security Services Platform provides centralized management for deploying and upgrading these components, performing compatibility verification, and ensuring integrity through MD5 verification for Security Intelligence appliance upgrades. Upgrade bundles are downloaded via HTTPS, and the platform tracks the installation status and versions of agents on bare metal servers, supporting controlled rollout operations.

While VCF and vDefend provide comprehensive secure update mechanisms for their respective infrastructure and security components, the explicit verification of update authenticity via digital signatures is not consistently detailed across all components. Furthermore, the management scope of both VCF and vDefend is limited to their own infrastructure and security components. Customers are responsible for implementing secure update mechanisms, including digital signature verification for authenticity, and managing the lifecycle of other embedded devices within their broader environment, such as IoT or OT equipment, which fall outside the direct management capabilities of VCF and vDefend.

Description:
Support for updates: Embedded device capability to support secure software/firmware updates.

Update authenticity: Verify authenticity of updates via digital signatures.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation and vDefend extensively utilize certificate-based authentication and digital signatures for their internal operations and component interactions. VMware Cloud Foundation employs certificates for internal service-to-service communication, programmatic access, and principal identity authentication within NSX, requiring SHA256-based certificates with RSA/DSA message algorithms and 2048-bit or above key sizes. VCF SSO supports holder-of-key SAML tokens, where authentication relies on security artifacts embedded in the token, and VCF Operations supports client certificate authentication for REST host connections and NSX principal identities. Similarly, vDefend's Security Services Platform components use certificates to identify clients and hosts, establish trust, secure communications, and control access levels, providing certificate management capabilities such as CSR generation and CA-signed certificate support.

These capabilities secure the platform's own internal service communications and authentication mechanisms.

However, the platform does not extend its enforcement or management of certificate-based authentication or digital signature verification to external embedded technologies, such as IoT or OT devices. Organizations are responsible for implementing dedicated IoT/OT identity and access management solutions to manage secure software/firmware updates and verify the authenticity of those updates via digital signatures for any external embedded devices within their environment.

Description:
Physical tamper resistance and detection (SI): Integrity-based tamper detection for hardware.

Hardware-based integrity: Use hardware (e.g., TPM) to verify device integrity.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes a robust foundation for hardware integrity verification by leveraging Trusted Platform Modules (TPM), Secure Boot, Intel Trusted Execution Technology (TXT), and confidential computing technologies such as AMD Secure Encrypted Virtualization (SEV) and Intel Trust Domain Extensions (TDX).

The platform supports the retrieval of TPM event logs to validate boot-time measurements, ensuring that the system's boot process remains untampered. vCenter actively monitors the attestation state of hosts, verifying that they have booted into known-good configurations and alerting on any deviations.

The ESX hypervisor enforces code signing to prevent the installation and execution of unauthorized code, further bolstering the integrity of the underlying hardware and software stack. VCF Operations continuously monitors the security state across the environment, detecting and alerting on deviations from established baselines.

While VCF provides comprehensive logical and hardware-assisted integrity verification, the physical assessment aspect of tamper resistance and detection falls outside the scope of a software platform. Organizations must implement physical security controls, including facility management, physical access procedures, and physical inspection of hardware components, to address the physical tamper resistance and detection requirements of this control.

Description:
Physical tamper resistance and detection (SI): Integrity-based tamper detection for hardware.

Hardware-based integrity: Use hardware (e.g., TPM) to verify device integrity.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) leverages hardware-level capabilities to establish and verify the integrity of its infrastructure components. VMware ESX enforces code signing to prevent the execution of unsigned or tampered binaries on the hypervisor. This is reinforced at the hardware level through Secure Boot, which validates each stage of the boot process against known-good signatures, and through Trusted Platform Module (TPM) 2.0 integration, which records boot measurements to detect changes to the boot chain. These mechanisms provide hardware-based integrity verification for the foundational software stack, contributing to the detection of tampering that would affect the boot process.

While VCF integrates with and utilizes hardware-level integrity features like TPM and Secure Boot, it does not directly provide physical tamper resistance mechanisms such as chassis intrusion detection, tamper-evident seals, or physical access controls for the underlying server hardware.

The platform's scope does not extend to direct management of firmware-level or microcontroller-level logging on server hardware, which remains dependent on the hardware vendor's baseboard management controller (BMC) and firmware capabilities.

Customers are responsible for implementing comprehensive physical security measures, including physical inspection of hardware components for signs of tampering, and establishing policies and procedures for physical access control and incident response related to physical security breaches.

Description: Provisioning Product: Ensure secure unique identity provisioning during manufacturing.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Integrity of boot process: Verify firmware integrity before execution.

Boot process authenticity: Verify firmware authenticity before execution.

Secure boot: Provide a hardware-rooted secure boot process.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes a robust hardware-rooted chain of trust from firmware through the hypervisor, directly addressing the integrity and authenticity of the boot process. VCF supports Secure Boot, Trusted Platform Module (TPM), and Intel Trusted Execution Technology (TXT) to verify that hosts boot in known-good configurations. vCenter monitors the host attestation state, and the hypervisor enforces code signing requirements, ensuring only trusted firmware and code execute.

Furthermore, VCF manages cryptographic keys through integration with KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers (KMS) or the built-in vSphere Native Key Provider. Trust between a KMS and vCenter is established through various methods, including uploading root CA certificates, exchanging certificates, or uploading signed certificates from the KMS vendor, with programmatic management available via the TrustedRootChains API.

While VCF provides the essential infrastructure for hardware-rooted secure boot, cryptographic key management, and trust establishment, organizations must configure their specific KMS integration and define key protection policies appropriate to their unique security requirements.

Description:
Integrity of boot process: Verify firmware integrity before execution.

Boot process authenticity: Verify firmware authenticity before execution.

Secure boot: Provide a hardware-rooted secure boot process.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation ensures the integrity of the ESXi hypervisor, a critical component of the boot process, through vSphere Lifecycle Manager. This platform enforces a desired-state software model for ESXi hosts, automatically validating and remediating any deviations from the approved software image specification when hosts are commissioned or join a cluster. This mechanism ensures that the ESXi software component of the boot chain remains authentic and untampered. VCF environments are designed to leverage the hardware-rooted secure boot capabilities provided by the underlying physical servers, enabling a more secure boot process for the virtualized infrastructure.

While VCF manages the integrity of the ESXi software and supports hardware-rooted secure boot, the ultimate provision of the hardware-rooted secure boot process and the initial verification of the physical server's firmware integrity before execution are functions of the underlying physical hardware and its configuration. Organizations are responsible for ensuring their physical servers are equipped with and configured to utilize secure boot mechanisms and for managing the integrity of the server's foundational firmware (e.g., BIOS/UEFI).

Description:
Integrity of boot process: Verify firmware integrity before execution.

Boot process authenticity: Verify firmware authenticity before execution.

Secure boot: Provide a hardware-rooted secure boot process.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation enforces digital signature verification for software and firmware components across multiple layers of the stack. At the hypervisor level, ESX leverages UEFI Secure Boot to verify that the boot loader and kernel modules are digitally signed before execution. The host acceptance level setting can be configured to accept only VMware-certified vSphere Installation Bundles (VIBs), restricting installation to components signed by VMware's certificate authority. vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) provides desired-state image management for ESX hosts, ensuring compliance against a defined image specification and preventing ad-hoc installation of unsigned or unapproved software components. During initial VCF deployment, components are assigned certificates, and communication between the VCF Installer and ESX hosts is performed over HTTPS with certificate identity validation. VCF Operations verifies that management pack PAK files are signed with a digital signature provided by VMware, preventing installation of unsigned or tampered packs. For container workloads, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) validates TLS certificates when fetching container images from registries, and VCF supports Harbor container registries which provide image signing and content trust capabilities.

While VCF provides the foundational capabilities for secure boot, firmware integrity, and authenticity verification, customers are responsible for enabling and configuring features such as UEFI Secure Boot and TPM on ESX hosts, as detailed in the vSphere Security Configuration Guide. Defining and enforcing host acceptance levels and target image specifications within vLCM are also customer responsibilities to ensure continuous compliance with approved software configurations.

Description:
Integrity of data at rest: Protect embedded storage from unauthorized modification.

Cryptographic integrity: Use cryptography to protect data at rest integrity.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust cryptographic integrity protection for data at rest associated with the boot process of both physical ESX hosts and virtual machines.

ESX hosts support UEFI Secure Boot, which employs cryptographic signature verification to validate each component loaded during the boot sequence, establishing a secure chain of trust from firmware through the hypervisor kernel. VCF further integrates with Trusted Platform Modules (TPM) on ESX hosts, enabling measured boot by recording cryptographic hashes of each boot component into platform configuration registers. These measurements are accessible via the trusted infrastructure API and can be leveraged by attestation services to validate that hosts have booted with known-good firmware and software configurations.

For virtual machines, VCF supports UEFI Secure Boot at the VM level, allowing guest operating systems to verify boot component integrity through the same cryptographic chain-of-trust mechanism used on physical hardware.

While VCF delivers the foundational cryptographic mechanisms and capabilities for integrity verification, customers are responsible for actively enabling UEFI Secure Boot on ESX hosts and virtual machines, configuring and integrating with attestation services to leverage TPM measurements for remote verification, and establishing the necessary policies and procedures for monitoring and responding to any detected integrity anomalies.

Description:
Integrity of data at rest: Protect embedded storage from unauthorized modification.

Cryptographic integrity: Use cryptography to protect data at rest integrity.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive, cryptographically-backed mechanisms to ensure the integrity of embedded storage and boot processes for both physical ESX hosts and virtual machines.

ESX hosts leverage UEFI Secure Boot, which employs cryptographic signature verification to validate each component loaded during the boot sequence, establishing a secure chain of trust from firmware through the hypervisor kernel. Furthermore, VCF integrates with Trusted Platform Modules (TPM) on ESX hosts, enabling measured boot. TPM devices record cryptographic hashes of each boot component into hardware-protected platform configuration registers, creating a tamper-evident record. vCenter provides programmatic access to TPM device information and event logs through the trusted infrastructure API, facilitating remote attestation to verify host boot integrity against expected values.

For virtual machines, VCF extends UEFI Secure Boot capabilities, allowing guest operating systems to verify boot component integrity through cryptographic chain-of-trust mechanisms. Additionally, VCF supports hardware memory encryption for confidential workloads via technologies like SEV-ES, protecting VM state from unauthorized inspection or modification at the hardware level.

While VMware Cloud Foundation delivers the foundational technical capabilities, customers are responsible for enabling and configuring these features according to their security policies.

This includes activating UEFI Secure Boot on ESX hosts and virtual machines, configuring TPM integration, and establishing an attestation service to compare TPM measurements against defined baselines for continuous integrity verification. The vSphere Security Configuration Guide provides recommendations for hardening, but the implementation of these recommendations and the ongoing management of cryptographic integrity controls fall within the customer's operational purview.

Description: Identification and Authentication Control: Requirement for all users/devices.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a robust identity and access management architecture centered on VCF SSO, which serves as the centralized authentication service for vCenter, SDDC Manager, NSX Manager, VCF Operations, and other platform components. This architecture supports identity federation with enterprise identity providers such as Microsoft Entra ID, ADFS, and Okta, enabling multifactor authentication and automatic user synchronization.

Role-based access control is comprehensively enforced across all VCF components, including vCenter, VCF Operations, SDDC Manager, and VCF Automation, allowing for both predefined and custom roles to implement least privilege.

VMware Kubernetes Service further extends these controls with namespace permission roles that map to Kubernetes ClusterRoles, supporting both VCF SSO and external OpenID Connect providers.

NSX integrates with LDAP and external identity management services for administrative access, complemented by network access policies.

Secure service-to-service communications between VCF components are established through certificate-based authentication.

VMware vDefend enhances these capabilities by providing identity-aware network enforcement via Identity Firewall, which integrates with Active Directory to apply firewall policies based on authenticated user identity, and implements RBAC within its own Security Services Platform and Security Intelligence management plane.

While the platform provides the comprehensive technical framework and tools for identification and authentication, the customer is responsible for configuring the integration with their chosen enterprise identity providers, defining specific organizational roles and permissions, and implementing access policies consistent with their security requirements. The customer must also manage the lifecycle of users and groups within their enterprise directory and ensure their external identity provider enforces any required multifactor authentication policies.

Description: Use Control: Requirement for enforcing least privilege and RBAC.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for enforcing least privilege and Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) through its foundational vCenter Server component. The platform's integrated vCenter Server offers a comprehensive RBAC framework, enabling the creation of custom roles with granular permissions. This allows administrators to define and assign access rights to users and groups from integrated identity sources, such as Active Directory or LDAP, ensuring that individuals and services operate with only the necessary privileges.

VCF components are deployed with service accounts configured for the minimum required permissions, inherently supporting the principle of least privilege.

While VCF delivers the technical mechanisms and infrastructure for RBAC and least privilege, the customer retains responsibility for defining specific organizational roles, establishing access policies, assigning users and groups to appropriate roles, and conducting regular audits of access assignments to ensure continuous adherence to least privilege principles.

Description: System Integrity: Requirement for maintaining hardware/software integrity.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust capabilities for maintaining hardware and software integrity across the infrastructure stack.

At the hardware layer, VCF supports Secure Boot, Trusted Platform Modules (TPM) attestation, and confidential computing technologies like AMD Secure Encrypted Virtualization (SEV-ES). vCenter retrieves TPM event logs and monitors host attestation to verify known good boot configurations. The ESX hypervisor enforces workload isolation, and VCF Operations verifies digital signatures on management pack PAK files. UEFI Secure Boot is supported for guest operating system integrity.

Configuration integrity is maintained through vSphere Configuration Profiles and vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM), which define desired states for ESX hosts, perform compliance checks, and remediate drift. VCF Operations extends this with scheduled configuration drift detection for vCenter instances and vSphere clusters, reporting deviations and maintaining an audit trail for configuration changes.

vSAN ensures data integrity through end-to-end checksums, automatic data repair, and RAID protection. For container workloads, VKS validates TLS certificates for images, and content libraries enforce security policies on VM images, with digital signature validation for OVF/OVA packages.

VCF Operations continuously monitors security posture through its dashboard, compliance checks, and alerting.

VMware vDefend further contributes to system integrity by protecting the network-facing attack surface of endpoints.

The Distributed Firewall enforces granular microsegmentation policies at the vNIC level, using L2-L7 filtering to restrict lateral movement. The Gateway Firewall extends this protection to north-south traffic.

vDefend IDS/IPS provides signature-based intrusion detection and prevention, detecting and blocking exploit attempts and command-and-control communications. The Malware Prevention Service adds network-inline file-based and fileless malware detection, including cloud sandboxing for unknown files.

Network Detection and Response (NDR) correlates events from these services to identify multi-stage attack campaigns, mapping detections to MITRE ATT&CK techniques. The Security Services Platform provides security segmentation scoring, offering visibility into endpoint protection posture.

While VCF provides extensive integrity and change-detection capabilities at the infrastructure and platform level, it does not include a native File Integrity Monitor (FIM) for individual files within guest operating systems or arbitrary files on VCF management appliances. Organizations must deploy a dedicated FIM solution for this specific requirement, which ESX Agent Manager can facilitate.

Furthermore, maintaining hardware and software integrity necessitates the customer's establishment of comprehensive security policies, operational procedures, and human accountability for defining desired states, monitoring alerts, and responding to detected integrity violations.

Description: Data Confidentiality: Requirement for protecting sensitive data.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation implements robust data confidentiality through multiple layers of protection. Data at rest is secured using vSAN encryption and per-VM encryption, both leveraging FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules and integrating with external key management systems for key lifecycle management. All data in transit between VCF components is protected by TLS, and vSphere Replication encrypts replication traffic between sites.

Access to operational data is controlled via privilege-based access controls that restrict visibility based on user roles. VMware vDefend enhances confidentiality by enforcing microsegmentation policies at the vNIC level through the Distributed Firewall, restricting unauthorized network access to systems containing sensitive data and preventing lateral movement.

Security Intelligence identifies and allows remediation of unencrypted data flows over risky protocols, while traffic flow analysis and anomaly detection identify potential data exfiltration attempts. vDefend's IDS/IPS inspects east-west traffic for data theft patterns, and Malware Prevention analyzes files for payloads targeting confidentiality.

VMware Live Site Recovery and vSAN data protection provide replication-based data protection, ensuring that data remains protected during recovery operations and across sites, with configurable policies and retention rules. VMware Kubernetes Service also includes a data protection extension for backup and restore operations.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical mechanisms, the customer is responsible for defining what constitutes sensitive data within their environment. This includes configuring and enabling the various encryption, access control, and network security features, establishing appropriate key management practices, and defining granular microsegmentation and replication policies. The customer must also implement organizational policies and procedures for data classification, handling, access management, and incident response to fully meet the overarching requirement for protecting sensitive data.

Description: Restricted Data Flow: Requirement for network segmentation.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, through VMware vDefend, provides comprehensive capabilities for network segmentation and enforcing restricted data flow across both East-West and North-South traffic. The vDefend Distributed Firewall enforces stateful Layer 2-7 security policies at the virtual network interface level of every workload, enabling microsegmentation by restricting workload-to-workload communication to only those flows explicitly permitted by policy. This includes a default deny rule that, when configured to drop or reject traffic, ensures a positive control model where only explicitly authorized traffic flows are permitted.

The vDefend Gateway Firewall extends this deny-by-default enforcement to the network edge for North-South traffic, supporting URL filtering for Layer 7 control.

NSX networking within VCF further supports a deny-by-default posture through routing infrastructure, where IP prefix lists on Tier-0 gateways default to a Deny action for routes.

VCF Automation facilitates consistent policy application by allowing security groups and associated firewall rules to be inherited by machines provisioned with network profiles.

Security Services Platform and Security Intelligence offer traffic flow analysis, automated rule recommendations, and a lockdown rule to assist administrators in transitioning to and maintaining a deny-by-default posture.

While the platform provides the robust infrastructure and tools for network segmentation, administrators are responsible for actively configuring the Distributed Firewall's default rule to a deny action (Drop or Reject) and then defining specific allowlist rules that permit only the necessary protocols, ports, and source-destination pairs for applications. The customer must craft and govern the specific microsegmentation policies and network access rules to achieve the required restricted data flow and network segmentation posture.

Description: Timely Response to Events: Requirement for audit and monitoring.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides the technical infrastructure for timely event response, including automated event detection, correlation, and fault surfacing with severity rankings from Info to Immediate. Notification rules can be configured to trigger actions based on incident state changes, and vCenter Server alarms execute scripts, send SNMP traps, or call APIs when infrastructure conditions match defined thresholds. VCF Operations includes a Troubleshooting Incidents page for creating, managing, and tracking incidents, complete with metadata such as status, impact, urgency, priority, and affected entities. Operators can flag metrics as root causes during investigation, and VCF integrates with ServiceNow for structured ITSM workflows.

For containment, vCenter Server supports isolating affected workloads through VM power state changes and network reconfiguration. Recovery capabilities include vSphere HA for automatic VM restarts on healthy hosts, Proactive HA for workload evacuation before hardware failures, and vSphere Fault Tolerance for zero-downtime protection.

VMware vDefend extends these capabilities by providing integrated threat detection, investigation, correlation, and remediation within the network security layer. Its Security Services Platform enables security teams to investigate individual threat events and correlated multi-stage attack campaigns, with Network Detection and Response (NDR) aggregating detection data and assigning impact scores based on confidence and severity. Security Intelligence displays suspicious traffic events categorized by impact type and severity, including Critical, to prioritize response efforts.

For remediation, vDefend Intelligent Assist offers AI-guided workflows to create targeted or comprehensive remediation policies, which are subject to human review and approval before taking effect. These policies can be applied immediately in the Emergency section of the vDefend Distributed Firewall for network-level containment.

VMware Live Recovery provides structured incident handling for disaster recovery scenarios through documented, repeatable recovery plans, non-disruptive testing, and automated failover orchestration. VLR's event logging and alarm framework detects data protection incidents, and recovery plan history reports document the outcomes of each execution, supporting post-incident review.

While these platforms provide robust technical tools for detection, analysis, containment, recovery, and the generation of auditable event data, they do not govern organization-wide incident response processes, maintain comprehensive incident response documentation, or manage all aspects of incident metadata such as status tracking, impact levels, and urgency classifications across the entire enterprise. The establishment of formal incident response policies, procedures, and the assignment of human accountability for timely response, audit, and monitoring remain the responsibility of the customer.

Description: Resource Availability: Requirement for DoS protection and backups.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation and VMware vDefend provide robust, multi-layered protection against denial of service attacks within the software-defined data center.

NSX Gateway and Segment QoS profiles enforce traffic rate limits and manage burst traffic, while broadcast rate limiting prevents broadcast storms. SpoofGuard provides anti-spoofing protection by validating MAC and IP addresses.

At the compute layer, vSphere Distributed Resource Scheduler (DRS) and resource pools prevent resource exhaustion by balancing workloads and allocating resources with reservations, limits, and shares. Network I/O Control (NIOC) prioritizes bandwidth for critical traffic types, and vSAN storage policies can limit IOPS per object. VCF Operations implements flow limit protection and API rate limiting.

VMware vDefend extends these capabilities with Distributed Firewall flood protection, specifically addressing TCP SYN flood attacks by limiting half-open connections. Its IDS/IPS engine detects and blocks known DoS attack patterns, and Malicious IP Filtering automatically blocks traffic from known threat sources. The Gateway Firewall can block traffic based on application-level information and URL reputation.

For resource availability and recovery, VMware Cloud Foundation delivers real-time and near real-time failover across all layers.

vSphere High Availability (HA) and Fault Tolerance ensure VM restart and zero-downtime failover, respectively. Proactive HA evacuates VMs from degrading hardware. vMotion and HCX enable live workload migration.

Network resilience is provided through multi-NIC teaming, LACP, and NSX gateway active-standby HA. Storage multipathing and vSAN stretched clusters, fault domains, and Express Storage Architecture provide robust storage-level resilience and site-level failover.

VMware Live Site Recovery automates site-level failover for critical workloads using recovery plans, supporting vSphere Replication, array-based replication, and Virtual Volumes policy-driven replication with configurable RPOs. It includes IP customization, test failover, and reprotect capabilities. vSAN Data Protection groups enable scheduled snapshot management for VM-level protection.

VMware vDefend ensures its own security infrastructure remains available through primary-passive HA for the Gateway Firewall and built-in HA for the Security Services Platform.

While the platform provides extensive internal DoS protection and robust availability and disaster recovery capabilities, it does not inherently provide perimeter-level volumetric DDoS mitigation, which requires integration with external scrubbing services or ISP-level solutions. Furthermore, while replication and snapshots contribute to recovery, a comprehensive backup strategy for long-term retention, granular file recovery, and protection against logical corruption typically requires dedicated backup software and processes to be implemented and managed by the customer. The customer is responsible for defining and implementing specific DoS protection policies, configuring replication and recovery plans, and establishing a complete backup and restoration strategy that aligns with their RTO/RPO objectives and data retention requirements.

Description:
Host physical tamper detection: Capability to detect host chassis tampering.

Host tamper response: Audit and alert upon host physical breach.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
Host physical tamper detection: Capability to detect host chassis tampering.

Host tamper response: Audit and alert upon host physical breach.
