Description:
Host malware protection: Protection from malicious code for host devices.

Centralized management: Centrally manage host malware signatures.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes a robust foundation for host malware protection through comprehensive platform integrity controls. ESX hosts enforce Secure Boot and code signing, preventing unauthorized code execution, while Trusted Platform Module (TPM) support and host attestation verify known good configurations. vSphere Lifecycle Manager and Configuration Profiles maintain desired-state configuration for ESX, detecting and remediating drift.

For containerized workloads, Harbor registry integration provides image signing, vulnerability scanning, and content trust.

Extending this, VMware vDefend Firewall with Advanced Threat Prevention delivers distributed malware detection and prevention. Its Malware Prevention Service, deployed as a service virtual machine, inspects files and data streams in real-time, employing both signature-based and behavioral analysis to detect known and zero-day threats, including fileless malware.

vDefend's IDS/IPS capabilities detect network-borne malware, command-and-control communications, and lateral movement, with support for custom signature bundles. The Malware Prevention Dashboard offers centralized visibility, aggregating detection events and tracking trends across the virtualized environment, thereby providing centralized management of threat intelligence and detection capabilities.

While VCF and vDefend provide strong preventative measures, platform integrity, and active detection and prevention of malicious code at the network and hypervisor layers, full eradication of malicious code from an already infected guest operating system typically requires dedicated endpoint security tools operating within the guest. Organizations must implement and manage these in-guest solutions to achieve complete remediation of compromised workloads.

Description:
Host malware protection: Protection from malicious code for host devices.

Centralized management: Centrally manage host malware signatures.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides mechanisms that contribute to the management of host security and updates. vCenter's update policy automates checks for new updates, including daily automatic checks, with automated staging and distribution across the infrastructure. vSphere Lifecycle Manager maintains software depots and supports scheduled synchronization of update metadata, ensuring hosts maintain a desired state configuration with drift detection and automated remediation. These capabilities help maintain a known good baseline across all hosts, supporting antimalware efforts by detecting unauthorized changes to host software.

VMware vDefend further contributes through its IDS/IPS signature bundle management and Malware Prevention Service signature downloads. Both operate with automated update capabilities, with IDS/IPS signatures automatically downloaded from vDefend Cloud every 24 hours and distributed via the Global Manager, and Malware Prevention Service signatures downloaded directly from the internet without manual intervention.

NSX Manager serves as the central management interface for deploying, configuring, and maintaining these network-based and hypervisor-layer malware prevention services, enabling consistent policy enforcement and centralized verdict distribution across the environment. Custom signature bundles for IDS/IPS can also be centrally managed, validated, and distributed.

However, VMware Cloud Foundation does not include a general-purpose antimalware engine with its own signature definitions for host devices.

Similarly, VMware vDefend's signature update capabilities are specific to network-level IDS/IPS and the Malware Prevention Service for file-based threat detection at the hypervisor layer; it does not replace a general-purpose endpoint antimalware product.

Therefore, the deployment, signature distribution, scan scheduling, and quarantine management for general-purpose endpoint antimalware technologies on host devices remain the responsibility of the organization's chosen security tooling.

Description:
Host update support: Capability to update host OS and applications.

Verified host updates: Verify authenticity/integrity of host updates.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive, integrated software and firmware patching across the entire infrastructure stack. SDDC Manager orchestrates lifecycle management for all VCF components, delivering time-sensitive fixes and managing patch releases.

The platform includes patch planner functionality for applying update binaries, performing pre-update validation with health checks and compatibility verification, and managing component interdependencies.

vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) manages software updates for ESX hosts, including base images, partner add-ons, IOVP drivers, and VMware Tools, utilizing dynamic patch baselines. Hardware Support Manager (HSM) plugins extend vLCM to unify firmware and driver updates.

The vCenter patching API supports discovery and pre-check operations for vCenter instances, while PowerCLI provides cmdlets for downloading patches, creating baselines, and scanning and remediating hosts, including a `Test-Compliance` command to verify entities against downloaded patches.

VCF supports staged rollouts, rollback capabilities, and Live Patching, ensuring data availability during updates. The VCF Download Tool facilitates patch management in offline environments.

VMware vDefend contributes to the overall security posture by providing network-level compensating controls that reduce exploitation risk during the window between vulnerability disclosure and patch deployment. While vDefend does not perform software or firmware patching, its Distributed IDS/IPS, Distributed Firewall, Malware Prevention, Advanced Threat Prevention, and Security Intelligence capabilities offer detection, isolation, and anomaly analysis to protect unpatched systems and inform patching priorities.

Description:
Host update support: Capability to update host OS and applications.

Verified host updates: Verify authenticity/integrity of host updates.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive capabilities for host update support and verification of update authenticity and integrity. vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) enables desired-state image management for ESX hosts, allowing administrators to define target configurations and remediate deviations, thereby ensuring hosts are updated to approved states. VCF enforces digital signature verification for software and firmware components across the stack. At the hypervisor level, ESX utilizes UEFI Secure Boot to validate digitally signed boot loaders and kernel modules prior to execution. The host acceptance level setting further restricts installation to VMware-certified vSphere Installation Bundles (VIBs), ensuring only components signed by VMware's certificate authority are permitted.

During initial VCF deployment, all components receive certificates from a default signing authority, and communication between the VCF Installer and ESX hosts is secured via HTTPS with certificate identity validation. VCF Operations verifies that management pack PAK files are signed with a valid VMware digital signature, preventing the installation of unsigned or tampered components. For containerized workloads, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) validates TLS certificates when fetching images from registries, requiring certificates to be signed by a trusted certificate authority. VCF supports Harbor container registries, which offer image signing and content trust features, and the VCF CLI imgpkg tool includes CA certificate validation for registry connections. These integrated mechanisms ensure the authenticity and integrity of all host updates and software components within the VCF environment.

Description:
Host integrity verification: Verify host hardware/software integrity.

Host hardware root of trust: Use hardware to anchor host integrity.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a robust host hardware root of trust through support for Secure Boot, Trusted Platform Modules (TPM), and Intel Trusted Execution Technology (TXT). It further incorporates confidential computing technologies such as AMD Secure Encrypted Virtualization (SEV) and Intel Trust Domain Extensions (TDX).

vCenter continuously monitors host attestation state, verifying that hosts boot into known-good configurations, and TPM event logs are retrievable for boot-time measurement validation.

The ESX hypervisor enforces code signing to prevent unauthorized code execution and isolates workloads from each other and the hypervisor.

VCF Operations continuously monitors the security state across the environment, alerting on deviations.

Logical integrity is maintained through vSphere Configuration Profiles and VCF Operations' Configuration Drifts feature, which proactively monitors and reports deviations from desired configurations with automatic checks every 8 hours.

vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) manages host software images and checks cluster compliance against defined specifications.

VCF Operations also provides compliance checking against security baselines and industry standards.

For virtual machines, Secure Boot provides guest operating system integrity verification, and VMware Tools packages are signed and verified during installation and updates.

Certificate validation continuously monitors validity and trust chains across VCF components.

Container workload integrity is supported through Harbor registry integration, which provides image signing, vulnerability scanning, and content trust for Kubernetes deployments.

vSAN ensures storage integrity with end-to-end checksums for data verification during read and write operations, automatically repairing mismatches.

While VMware Cloud Foundation provides extensive logical integrity assessment and hardware root of trust capabilities, the physical assessment of hardware and system components for signs of physical tampering falls outside the scope of the software platform.

Organizations must implement comprehensive physical security controls through facility management and physical access procedures to address this aspect of host hardware integrity.

Description:
Host integrity verification: Verify host hardware/software integrity.

Host hardware root of trust: Use hardware to anchor host integrity.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) Operations provides comprehensive capabilities for verifying host software integrity. It continuously monitors the managed environment, generating alerts for anomalous conditions, metric deviations from dynamic and static thresholds, and surfacing relevant events and property changes. Audit event reporting records configuration changes, user actions, and authentication events across the managed infrastructure, offering detailed filtering for investigation. VCF Operations continuously monitors infrastructure against security baselines, detecting compliance violations and identifying non-compliant objects. Configuration drift detection compares current settings against desired-state templates, notifying administrators of any deviations that could indicate unauthorized modification. ESX hosts generate detailed audit logs, and multiple VCF components support syslog forwarding to external log aggregation and SIEM systems, including vSphere Supervisor forwarding control plane and Kubernetes API server audit logs. VCF Operations for Networks monitors network flows and detects anomalous traffic patterns, with NSX supporting integration with external logging and monitoring solutions for security event correlation. These capabilities provide the telemetry and change-detection foundation that supports an IoC inspection program for software and configuration integrity.

While VCF provides extensive software integrity verification, it does not inherently verify host hardware integrity or establish a hardware root of trust. Physical inspection of hardware and system components for signs of physical tampering, as well as the implementation of hardware-anchored integrity mechanisms such as Trusted Platform Module (TPM) for secure boot and attestation, falls outside the platform's direct scope. Customers are responsible for implementing physical security controls, establishing processes for hardware integrity verification, and deploying solutions that leverage hardware roots of trust to anchor host integrity. A complete IoC detection program also requires external tools such as a SIEM or endpoint detection platform, and the definition of associated policies and procedures.

Description: Host provisioning: Securely provision host unique identifiers.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes a robust foundation for host security through cryptographic key management and hardware-based integrity verification. VCF integrates with KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers (KMS) or utilizes the built-in vSphere Native Key Provider for managing cryptographic keys. Trust between vCenter and a KMS is established via certificate exchange, supporting root, intermediate, or leaf CA certificates, with programmatic management available through the TrustedRootChains API.

At the hardware level, VCF supports Secure Boot, Trusted Platform Module (TPM), and Intel Trusted Execution Technology (TXT), ensuring hosts boot into known-good configurations. vCenter actively monitors host attestation states, and the hypervisor enforces code signing requirements, thereby establishing a hardware-rooted chain of trust from firmware through the hypervisor.

While VCF provides the essential infrastructure for key management and trust establishment, the secure provisioning of host unique identifiers ultimately requires customer action. Organizations must configure their specific KMS integration, define comprehensive key protection policies tailored to their security requirements, and implement processes to ensure the secure generation, assignment, and protection of host unique identifiers within the VCF environment.

Description:
Host boot integrity: Verify integrity of the host OS bootloader.

Host boot authenticity: Verify authenticity of the host OS bootloader.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a robust foundation for verifying host boot integrity and authenticity through hardware-rooted trust mechanisms. The platform supports Secure Boot, Trusted Platform Module (TPM), and Intel Trusted Execution Technology (TXT), which are critical for ensuring hosts boot into known-good configurations. vCenter actively monitors the attestation state of hosts, and the hypervisor enforces code signing requirements, thereby establishing a hardware-rooted chain of trust that extends from firmware through the hypervisor.

Furthermore, VCF integrates with KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers (KMS) or utilizes the built-in vSphere Native Key Provider for cryptographic key management, and allows for the establishment of trust with enterprise or third-party certificate authorities through the vCenter trusted certificate store and the TrustedRootChains API.

While VMware Cloud Foundation provides the essential infrastructure for key management and trust establishment, organizations are responsible for configuring their KMS integration, enabling and configuring hardware-level security features such as Secure Boot and TPM on their hosts, and defining key protection policies appropriate to their specific security requirements.

Description:
Host boot integrity: Verify integrity of the host OS bootloader.

Host boot authenticity: Verify authenticity of the host OS bootloader.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a robust hardware-rooted chain of trust to verify the integrity and authenticity of the host OS bootloader. The platform integrates with hardware-level security features such as Secure Boot, Trusted Platform Module (TPM), and Intel Trusted Execution Technology (TXT). Secure Boot ensures that only cryptographically signed code, including the bootloader, is executed during the boot process, thereby verifying its integrity and authenticity. TPM provides a hardware root of trust for cryptographic operations and stores measurements of boot components, enabling remote attestation of the host's boot state. Intel TXT further enhances the trusted launch process by creating a measured and verified execution environment. vCenter Server continuously monitors the attestation state of hosts, providing visibility into their boot integrity, and the hypervisor enforces code signing requirements for critical components, ensuring that only trusted software runs.

While VMware Cloud Foundation provides the foundational infrastructure and tools for boot integrity and authenticity verification, organizations are responsible for enabling and configuring these capabilities. This includes activating Secure Boot on hosts, configuring and managing TPMs, establishing attestation policies within vCenter, and defining appropriate responses to attestation failures. Organizations must also integrate with external Key Management Servers if required for broader cryptographic key management, and establish their own certificate trust chains for validating boot components and attestation reports.

Description:
Host data at rest integrity: Protect host file system integrity.

Host storage encryption: Use cryptography for host data at rest.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive mechanisms to ensure the integrity of host and virtual machine boot processes. ESXi hosts leverage UEFI Secure Boot to cryptographically verify each component loaded during the boot sequence, establishing a chain of trust from firmware through the hypervisor kernel. This capability is recommended for all ESXi hosts as part of standard hardening. Furthermore, VCF integrates with Trusted Platform Modules (TPM) on ESXi hosts, enabling measured boot where cryptographic hashes of boot components are recorded. vCenter provides programmatic access to TPM event logs, facilitating remote attestation services to validate that hosts boot with known-good firmware and software configurations, thereby detecting tampering or unauthorized changes. For virtual machines, VCF extends UEFI Secure Boot capabilities, allowing guest operating systems to verify boot component integrity through a similar cryptographic chain of trust.

While VCF provides robust integrity verification for the boot process, the implementation of host storage encryption using cryptography for all data at rest on the host file system is a separate customer responsibility. The customer must configure and enable appropriate encryption solutions, such as vSAN encryption, VM encryption, or leveraging underlying storage array encryption, to meet the requirement for host data at rest encryption. This also includes defining and enforcing policies for key management and cryptographic controls for all host-level data.

Description:
Host data at rest integrity: Protect host file system integrity.

Host storage encryption: Use cryptography for host data at rest.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive capabilities to protect the integrity of host file systems and boot processes. ESX hosts leverage UEFI Secure Boot, which cryptographically verifies each component in the boot chain, establishing a chain of trust from firmware through the hypervisor kernel. This mechanism prevents unauthorized or tampered code from executing. Furthermore, VCF integrates with Trusted Platform Modules (TPM) on ESX hosts to perform measured boot, recording cryptographic hashes of all boot components into tamper-evident platform configuration registers. vCenter provides programmatic access to these TPM measurements, enabling remote attestation services to validate host boot integrity against expected configurations and detect any discrepancies.

For virtual machines, VCF extends UEFI Secure Boot capabilities to the VM level, ensuring guest operating system boot integrity through similar cryptographic signature verification. VCF also supports hardware memory encryption for confidential workloads via technologies like SEV-ES, protecting VM state in memory from inspection or modification at the hardware level.

While VCF establishes a robust foundation for host and virtual machine integrity and provides encryption for VM memory, the comprehensive application of cryptography for all host data at rest, including the ESXi boot device and other host-level storage, requires customer configuration and policy enforcement. Organizations must ensure that host storage encryption is implemented through appropriate VCF features or integrated storage solutions to fully meet the requirement for host data at rest encryption.

Description:
Untrusted network access: Control network traffic from untrusted zones.

Network access control (NAC): Implement NAC for untrusted devices.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, leveraging NSX, provides robust network segmentation capabilities, including micro-segmentation and tenant-level isolation through NSX VPC and projects. This establishes a foundational layer for controlling network traffic from untrusted zones.

VMware vDefend further enforces this by treating all east-west traffic as untrusted by default, utilizing its Distributed Firewall to apply security policies at the vNIC level. This default-deny posture mandates explicit authorization for all communication, effectively preventing unauthorized lateral movement.

For network access control, vDefend's Identity Firewall integrates with Active Directory, enabling the creation of firewall rules based on user identity and group membership. This allows for granular, identity-aware access control, ensuring that only explicitly authorized identities and their associated devices can access specific resources, thereby implementing a form of network access control for untrusted devices.

The Security Services Platform guides the implementation of these zero trust principles through a structured security segmentation journey and provides intelligence to identify and remediate policy gaps.

While the platform delivers the technical enforcement mechanisms for controlling untrusted network access and implementing network access control, the customer is responsible for defining the specific security policies, establishing identity governance, and implementing continuous verification processes. These organizational policies and operational procedures are essential to fully realize the control's intent and establish a comprehensive Zero Trust Architecture.

Description:
Untrusted network access: Control network traffic from untrusted zones.

Network access control (NAC): Implement NAC for untrusted devices.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) and VMware vDefend provide robust technical capabilities to control network traffic from untrusted zones and implement network access control (NAC) principles for untrusted devices.

VCF enforces authentication for all management functions, utilizing centralized Single Sign-On with multi-factor authentication and OAuth 2.0, and maintains a least-privilege authorization model. NSX within VCF delivers comprehensive network segmentation, including micro-segmentation and tenant-level isolation through NSX VPC and projects, extending consistent security boundaries to containerized workloads via NSX-backed network security policies.

VMware vDefend, a foundational component for Zero Trust Architecture, enforces security policies at the vNIC level with its Distributed Firewall, treating all east-west traffic as untrusted by default and requiring explicit authorization for communication. This default-deny posture prevents unauthorized lateral movement.

The Security Services Platform guides administrators through a structured security segmentation journey, progressing from broad infrastructure segmentation to fine-grained application ringfencing.

Security Intelligence enhances this by visualizing network flows, identifying unprotected traffic, and generating firewall rule recommendations to systematically close security gaps.

Furthermore, vDefend Identity Firewall integrates with Active Directory, enabling the creation of firewall rules based on user identity and group membership, thereby extending access control beyond IP addresses to specific user populations across both Distributed and Gateway Firewalls. This capability directly supports the implementation of identity-aware network access control for devices and users.

While the platform provides the essential technical enforcement mechanisms for controlling untrusted network access and implementing NAC, the organization remains responsible for defining specific security policies, establishing identity governance, and implementing continuous verification processes. The customer must configure the granular firewall rules, define trust zones, and manage user identities and groups within Active Directory to fully realize the intended network access controls.

Description: Network malware protection: Filter malicious code at network boundaries.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides the robust network infrastructure, powered by NSX, which establishes the foundational segmentation, routing, and distributed architecture necessary for advanced security services. VMware vDefend, an integral extension of VCF's networking capabilities, directly implements comprehensive malicious code filtering at network boundaries.

vDefend's Advanced Threat Prevention, integrated with the Gateway Firewall, delivers native Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDS/IPS) capabilities that operate at perimeter gateway enforcement points, inspecting north-south traffic for network-borne malware delivery attempts, command-and-control communications, and lateral movement. This is complemented by a Malicious IP Feed that automatically blocks known malicious sources and App ID classification to restrict traffic to approved protocols. Furthermore, the vDefend Malware Prevention Service, deployed as a service virtual machine, inspects files and data streams in real-time for malicious content, including zero-day and fileless threats, utilizing both signature-based detection and behavioral analysis. The Gateway Firewall also incorporates Cloud Sandboxing and Artifact Analysis, which detonates and analyzes potentially malicious files traversing the network in an isolated environment to generate automated verdicts.

While vDefend provides the full technical capability to filter malicious code at network boundaries, customers are responsible for enabling and configuring these advanced threat prevention features, including the deployment of service virtual machines, activation of IDS/IPS across clusters, and the definition of custom signatures or policies. Ongoing monitoring of security dashboards and alerts is also a customer operational responsibility.

Although vDefend effectively prevents malicious code from entering or spreading across the network, complete eradication of malware from already infected guest operating systems typically necessitates the use of endpoint security tools operating within the guest environment.

Description:
Network device updates: Support secure updates for network device firmware.

Verified network updates: Cryptographically verify network firmware updates.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides integrated software and firmware patching across the entire infrastructure stack, including network-enabled components such as ESXi hosts and NSX-T. SDDC Manager orchestrates lifecycle management for all VCF components, delivering time-sensitive fixes and managing update binaries. VCF Operations includes patch planner functionality, pre-update validation for health and compatibility, and component interdependency management to ensure robust and secure update processes. vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) manages software updates for ESX hosts, including the ESX base image, partner add-ons, and VMware Tools. Hardware Support Manager (HSM) plugins extend vLCM to provide unified lifecycle management for vendor-specific firmware and driver updates, ensuring that firmware for network-enabled hardware components is managed alongside software patches.

VCF's update mechanisms, including SDDC Manager and vLCM, inherently verify the cryptographic signatures of VMware-provided updates for its core components, such as ESXi hosts and NSX-T, ensuring the authenticity and integrity of network device firmware before application. For vendor-specific firmware managed via HSM plugins, VCF orchestrates the update process, relying on the integrated vendor tools and underlying hardware to perform the necessary cryptographic verification.

The platform supports staged rollouts, rollback capabilities, and live patching for discrete components, maintaining availability during updates.

VMware vDefend further enhances the security posture by providing network-level compensating controls that reduce exploitation risk during the window between vulnerability disclosure and patch deployment.

While vDefend does not perform software or firmware patching, its Distributed IDS/IPS detects exploitation attempts against known vulnerabilities, with signature updates for specific CVEs and the ability to author custom signatures for virtual patching. The Distributed Firewall enables microsegmentation to isolate unpatched or vulnerable workloads, limiting lateral movement and reducing the blast radius. Malware Prevention and Advanced Threat Prevention (ATP) provide an additional detection layer for exploits targeting unpatched vulnerabilities, including sandboxing for behavioral analysis of zero-day threats. Security Intelligence offers traffic flow analysis and anomaly detection to identify unusual network patterns indicative of exploitation activity.

The customer is responsible for defining and implementing security policies that leverage vDefend's capabilities, such as configuring IDS/IPS signatures, establishing microsegmentation policies, and responding to alerts generated by ATP and Security Intelligence. These actions are crucial for maximizing the effectiveness of vDefend as a compensating control.

Description:
Network device updates: Support secure updates for network device firmware.

Verified network updates: Cryptographically verify network firmware updates.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides integrated lifecycle management through SDDC Manager and vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) to manage updates and patches across the entire VCF stack. This includes the secure management of firmware and drivers for ESXi hosts and their network adapters via Hardware Support Manager (HSM) plugins, ensuring hosts remain on supported hardware and software combinations.

The update process incorporates pre-update health checks, compatibility verification, and component interdependency management to ensure updates are applied securely and in the correct sequence. VCF supports zero-downtime maintenance, staged rollouts, and rollback capabilities, enhancing the security and reliability of these updates.

Configuration Profiles, Host Profiles, and Image-Based Lifecycle Management continuously assess host configuration against a desired state, detecting and remediating drift in software and firmware versions.

While VCF provides robust mechanisms for managing and securely updating firmware for its core infrastructure components, the responsibility for managing and cryptographically verifying firmware updates for external network devices (e.g., physical switches, routers, firewalls) that support the VCF environment typically falls to the customer. The customer must also establish organizational policies for update schedules, approval processes, and verification of cryptographic signatures for all relevant network device firmware.

Description:
Network device integrity: Verify integrity of network device configuration.

Hardware integrity (Network): Use hardware-based integrity for network assets.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for verifying the integrity of network device configurations. VCF Operations continuously monitors infrastructure against security baselines, detects compliance violations, and identifies non-compliant objects by resource and violation type. Configuration drift detection compares current settings against desired-state templates, notifying administrators of any deviations that could indicate unauthorized modification. Audit event reporting records configuration changes, user actions, and authentication events across the managed infrastructure, providing detailed views of activity. VCF Operations for Networks monitors network flows and can detect anomalous traffic patterns, and NSX supports integration with external logging and monitoring solutions for security event correlation. VMware vDefend further addresses the logical, network-layer dimension of integrity by continuously inspecting traffic through IDS/IPS signature matching, including content inspection at specified byte offsets, and recording event details to support periodic review of suspicious traffic. Security Intelligence Suspicious Traffic events capture MITRE ATT&CK tactics and techniques associated with detected anomalous behavior, enabling analysts to identify network-level indicators of compromise across workloads.

While VCF provides extensive software-defined and logical network integrity verification, the physical inspection of hardware and system components for signs of physical tampering, and the implementation of hardware-based integrity mechanisms for network assets, are outside the scope of the platform.

Customers must implement physical security controls and establish processes to verify the hardware integrity of their physical network infrastructure and the underlying hardware components of the VCF environment. Additionally, customers are responsible for defining desired-state configurations, establishing security baselines, configuring alerts, and implementing a comprehensive IoC detection program that integrates VCF telemetry with external SIEM or endpoint detection platforms.

Description:
Network device integrity: Verify integrity of network device configuration.

Hardware integrity (Network): Use hardware-based integrity for network assets.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) Operations provides comprehensive capabilities for verifying network device configuration integrity. It continuously monitors the managed environment, generating alerts for anomalous conditions and metric deviations from dynamic and static thresholds.

Configuration drift detection compares current settings against desired-state templates, notifying administrators of any deviations that could indicate unauthorized modification. Audit event reporting records configuration changes, user actions, and authentication events across the managed infrastructure, offering a detailed view of activity that supports integrity verification.

VCF Operations for Networks monitors network flows and can detect anomalous traffic patterns, while NSX supports integration with external logging and monitoring solutions for security event correlation.

VMware vDefend further addresses the logical, network-layer dimension by continuously inspecting traffic through IDS/IPS signature matching, including content inspection, and recording event details to support periodic review. Security Intelligence Suspicious Traffic events capture MITRE ATT&CK tactics and techniques associated with detected anomalous behavior, enabling analysts to identify network-level indicators of compromise across workloads.

While VCF provides robust capabilities for verifying network device configuration integrity and detecting anomalous network behavior, the physical inspection of hardware and system components for signs of physical tampering is outside the platform's scope. The implementation of hardware-based integrity for network assets and physical security controls to prevent tampering must be addressed through customer-defined policies and operational procedures.

Description: Network provisioning: Securely provision network device identities.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a robust foundation for securing device identities through comprehensive cryptographic key management and hardware-based integrity verification. VCF integrates with KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers (KMS) or utilizes the built-in vSphere Native Key Provider to manage cryptographic keys, which are fundamental to device identity. Trust between vCenter and a KMS is established through secure certificate exchange, including the ability to upload root CA, intermediate CA, or leaf certificates. Furthermore, VCF allows for the management of trusted root certificate chains within the vCenter trusted certificate store, enabling trust with enterprise or third-party certificate authorities for identity issuance.

At the hardware level, VCF supports Secure Boot, Trusted Platform Module (TPM), and Intel Trusted Execution Technology (TXT) to ensure that ESXi hosts boot into known-good configurations. vCenter actively monitors the attestation state of hosts, and the hypervisor enforces code signing requirements, thereby establishing a hardware-rooted chain of trust from firmware through the hypervisor for its compute infrastructure.

While VMware Cloud Foundation provides the essential infrastructure for key management and trust establishment, organizations are responsible for configuring their specific KMS integrations, defining appropriate key protection policies, and implementing processes for the broader provisioning and lifecycle management of network device identities across their environment.

Description:
Network boot integrity: Verify network device bootloader integrity.

Network boot authenticity: Verify network device bootloader authenticity.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a robust hardware-rooted chain of trust for its ESXi hosts, directly addressing network device bootloader integrity and authenticity. The platform integrates with KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers (KMS) or utilizes the vSphere Native Key Provider for cryptographic key management, enabling the establishment of trust through various certificate-based methods, including root CA certificates, certificate exchange, or vendor-signed certificates. For enhanced security, administrators can upload leaf or intermediate CA certificates to the vCenter trusted certificate store, which also supports programmatic management via the TrustedRootChains API.

At the hardware level, VCF leverages Secure Boot, Trusted Platform Module (TPM), and Intel Trusted Execution Technology (TXT) to ensure hosts boot into known-good configurations. vCenter actively monitors host attestation states, and the hypervisor enforces code signing requirements, thereby verifying the integrity and authenticity of the boot process from firmware through the hypervisor.

While VMware Cloud Foundation provides the essential infrastructure for key management and trust establishment, organizations are responsible for configuring their specific KMS integration and defining key protection policies tailored to their unique security requirements.

Description:
Network boot integrity: Verify network device bootloader integrity.

Network boot authenticity: Verify network device bootloader authenticity.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) contributes to verifying network device bootloader integrity and authenticity by enforcing a desired-state software model for ESXi hosts. vSphere Lifecycle Manager ensures that all ESXi hosts within a cluster adhere to an approved software image specification, which includes the base image and its boot components. When hosts are commissioned or join a cluster, vLCM validates their configuration against this desired state and automatically remediates any deviations, thereby ensuring the integrity and authenticity of the ESXi host's boot process and software.

This capability, however, does not extend to dedicated physical network equipment such as switches, routers, or access points. For these components, organizations must implement vendor-specific Zero-Touch Provisioning (ZTP) frameworks or external orchestration tools to verify their bootloader integrity and authenticity.

VMware vDefend primarily automates the application of network security policies to devices as they are onboarded, rather than verifying the integrity or authenticity of their bootloaders.

Organizations are responsible for defining the approved software image specifications for ESXi hosts and for implementing and managing external solutions for physical network device bootloader verification.

This is further enhanced by Trusted Platform Module (TPM) integration, which performs measured boot and records cryptographic hashes of boot components into tamper-evident registers, enabling remote attestation to verify host boot integrity against expected configurations. Description:
Zone boundary protection: Control flow between different security zones.

Deny by default: Implement a "deny all, permit by exception" policy.

Stateful inspection: Use stateful traffic filtering at boundaries.

Fail close: Ensure boundary protection fails to a secure (closed) state.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes the foundational networking infrastructure for zone boundary protection through NSX and vSphere Distributed Switches, enabling logical network segmentation via NSX segments, VLANs, and Private VLANs.
NSX edge gateways manage North-South traffic, providing routing, NAT, and VPN connectivity at the perimeter.

VMware vDefend extends this foundation by providing stateful firewall inspection and enforcement at both external and internal boundaries.
The vDefend Gateway Firewall enforces zone boundaries and performs stateful North-South traffic inspection at the network perimeter, while the Distributed Firewall enforces East-West traffic policies at the vNIC level, enabling microsegmentation and creating internal boundary controls.

Security Services Platform Protection Rules further enforce environment-level boundaries with explicit Drop or Reject actions, directly supporting a "deny all, permit by exception" policy.
Security Intelligence provides visibility into traffic crossing defined internal and external boundaries, and Network Detection and Response (NDR) monitors traffic patterns for threat identification.

While the platform provides robust technical controls for zone boundary protection, deny-by-default policy enforcement, and stateful inspection, the specific mechanisms ensuring boundary protection fails to a secure (closed) state are not explicitly detailed as a platform capability.

Furthermore, the organization is responsible for defining security zones, establishing boundary placement decisions, implementing the "deny all, permit by exception" policy, and managing firewall rule sets.
Organizational processes for reviewing traffic logs and ongoing management of security policies are also required.

Description:
Zone boundary protection: Control flow between different security zones.

Deny by default: Implement a "deny all, permit by exception" policy.

Stateful inspection: Use stateful traffic filtering at boundaries.

Fail close: Ensure boundary protection fails to a secure (closed) state.
Response:
VMware vDefend provides comprehensive capabilities for zone boundary protection, implementing a deny-by-default posture, and stateful traffic inspection. The vDefend Gateway Firewall enforces North-South zone boundaries with stateful inspection at the network perimeter, while the Distributed Firewall applies East-West traffic policies at the vNIC level, enabling microsegmentation and creating granular internal boundary controls. Security Services Platform further enhances boundary enforcement through Protection Rules, which can be configured to explicitly drop or reject all traffic between specified environment pairs, thereby enforcing a deny-by-default policy. Security Intelligence identifies and recommends protective policies for external communications, reinforcing boundary security. Network Detection and Response (NDR) monitors traffic patterns across these boundaries for threat identification. The underlying NSX networking infrastructure, provided by VMware Cloud Foundation, establishes the segmentation and routing fabric upon which vDefend's advanced enforcement mechanisms operate.

While vDefend provides the technical mechanisms for boundary enforcement, the organization is responsible for defining the specific security zones, establishing the overarching boundary placement decisions, and creating the detailed security policies and firewall rule sets that govern traffic flow.

Description:
Zone boundary protection: Control flow between different security zones.

Deny by default: Implement a "deny all, permit by exception" policy.

Stateful inspection: Use stateful traffic filtering at boundaries.

Fail close: Ensure boundary protection fails to a secure (closed) state.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes robust zone boundary protection through its integrated networking infrastructure, leveraging NSX and vSphere Distributed Switches to create multiple layers of network segmentation and traffic control. VCF defines and enforces boundaries using NSX segments, VLANs, Private VLANs, and dedicated VMkernel port configurations that isolate management, storage, and vMotion traffic.

NSX edge gateways manage North-South traffic, supporting routing, NAT, and VPN, while Virtual Private Clouds (VPCs) provide self-contained, isolated network environments. Network I/O Control maintains performance boundaries, and VCF Automation provides isolated organizations and projects with dedicated network configurations.

VMware vDefend significantly enhances these capabilities by providing stateful firewall inspection and microsegmentation at both internal and perimeter network boundaries.

The vDefend Gateway Firewall enforces zone boundaries and performs stateful North-South traffic inspection at the network perimeter, applying policies to traffic entering and leaving Tier-0 and Tier-1 gateways. The vDefend Distributed Firewall enforces East-West traffic policies at the vNIC level, creating per-workload enforcement points that function as internal boundary controls, supporting a "deny all, permit by exception" policy model through its rule processing logic and explicit block/reject actions for environment pairs.

Security Services Platform further extends environment-level boundary enforcement with Protection Rules to control traffic flow between logical environments, and Security Intelligence provides visibility into cross-boundary traffic and recommends protective policies.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical mechanisms for boundary protection, stateful inspection, and deny-by-default policy enforcement, the definition of security zones, the establishment of boundary placement decisions, and the creation of specific "deny all, permit by exception" policies remain organizational architecture and governance responsibilities. Furthermore, the provided capabilities do not explicitly detail how boundary protection mechanisms ensure a "fail close" state in the event of a system or component failure, which is a critical aspect of secure boundary design.

Description:
Zone boundary protection: Control flow between different security zones.

Deny by default: Implement a "deny all, permit by exception" policy.

Stateful inspection: Use stateful traffic filtering at boundaries.

Fail close: Ensure boundary protection fails to a secure (closed) state.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes the foundational networking infrastructure for zone boundary protection, leveraging NSX and vSphere Distributed Switches to provide multiple layers of network segmentation. This includes NSX segments, VLANs, Private VLANs, and VMkernel port separation, which create logical network boundaries and isolate traffic at Layer 2. NSX edge gateways manage North-South traffic at the perimeter, supporting routing, NAT, and VPN connectivity, while Virtual Private Clouds offer project-level boundary isolation.

VMware vDefend extends these capabilities by providing active enforcement and stateful inspection. The vDefend Gateway Firewall enforces zone boundaries and performs stateful North-South traffic inspection at the network perimeter, applying policies to traffic entering and leaving Tier-0 and Tier-1 gateways. The vDefend Distributed Firewall enforces East-West traffic policies between workloads at the vNIC level, enabling microsegmentation and creating fine-grained internal boundary controls.

The Security Services Platform further enhances this with environment-level boundary enforcement through Protection Rules, which define and control traffic flow between environment pairs. Security Intelligence provides visibility into traffic crossing defined boundaries and identifies workloads with external communications, recommending protective policies. The recommended configuration for the Distributed Firewall default rule is to block, establishing a "deny all, permit by exception" policy for network traffic.

For fail-secure operation, vDefend ensures security enforcement continuity during infrastructure failures and workload migrations. The Distributed Firewall's architectural placement at the hypervisor kernel means firewall rules remain bound to each workload, traveling with it during vMotion without interruption to protection. The default-deny posture of the Distributed Firewall inherently provides fail-secure behavior, as any failure in rule delivery or policy lookup results in traffic being denied.

The vDefend Gateway Firewall on NSX Edge clusters provides stateful failover, pre-synchronizing flow state tables on the new active node to preserve TCP connections and sessions through switchover events. NSX Federation enables disaster recovery for distributed and gateway firewall policies across multiple locations, ensuring the established security framework remains enforced during site-level failure scenarios.

VMware Cloud Foundation contributes to overall system resilience, with vSphere Fault Tolerance providing continuous availability and vSphere High Availability automatically restarting VMs on surviving hosts, ensuring workloads return to a known-good state. vSAN fault domains provide data resilience, and vSphere Lifecycle Manager can remediate hosts back to their defined configuration.

VMware Live Recovery ensures deterministic states during disaster recovery operations, handling split-brain scenarios by flagging inconsistencies to prevent data compromise and preserving system state information through recovery plan history and point-in-time replication snapshots.

While the platform provides the necessary technical mechanisms, the organization is responsible for defining its specific security zones, establishing boundary placement decisions, and creating the policies that govern traffic flow. This includes configuring the "deny all, permit by exception" policy, defining and managing firewall rule sets, and implementing organizational processes for reviewing traffic logs and managing security configurations.

Description: Network communication restriction: Block general purpose person-to-person traffic.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, through its integrated NSX and vDefend components, provides the essential network security enforcement layer to restrict general-purpose person-to-person traffic.

The NSX Distributed Firewall operates with a default-deny posture at the virtual machine's network interface (vNIC) level, ensuring that all east-west traffic is blocked unless explicitly allowed by a security policy. This micro-segmentation capability prevents unauthorized lateral movement by treating all internal communications as untrusted by default.

The vDefend Identity Firewall further refines this by integrating with Active Directory, enabling the creation of security policies based on user identity and group membership. This allows for precise control over which users or groups are authorized to communicate, directly addressing the "person-to-person" aspect of traffic restriction by permitting only explicitly sanctioned interactions.

While the platform provides the robust technical mechanisms for enforcing these restrictions, the customer is responsible for defining what constitutes "general purpose person-to-person traffic" within their specific operational context. This includes identifying the specific protocols, applications, and user groups that must be restricted. The customer must then create, implement, and maintain the appropriate Distributed Firewall and Identity Firewall rules to enforce these policies, manage the underlying identity infrastructure, and continuously monitor network activity to ensure compliance and refine policies as needed.

Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) rigorously enforces the integrity and authenticity of its platform components and containerized workloads.

At the hypervisor level, ESX leverages UEFI Secure Boot to verify digital signatures of boot loaders and kernel modules, while host acceptance levels restrict vSphere Installation Bundles (VIBs) to VMware-certified components. vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) maintains desired-state compliance for ESX hosts, preventing the installation of unsigned or unapproved software.

During deployment, VCF validates component certificates and secures communication via HTTPS. For container workloads, VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) validates TLS certificates for image registries, requiring trusted certificate authority signatures, and VCF supports Harbor container registries which provide image signing and content trust capabilities.

This robust framework ensures that the underlying infrastructure and container images are authentic and untampered. Furthermore, VCF's support for containerized applications inherently provides application sandboxing by running code in restricted containers.

While VCF provides a secure foundation for application execution and verifies the integrity of its own components and container images, the enforcement of specific security policies *within* customer applications and the definition of application-level authorization for execution remain the responsibility of the customer.

Customers must configure and utilize the provided container security features, such as Harbor content trust, and implement appropriate application design and configuration to meet their specific application security policy and authorization requirements.

Description: Application malware protection: Use app-level scanning for malicious code.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes a robust foundation that significantly hinders malware persistence.

Platform integrity controls, including Secure Boot and code signing enforced at the ESX hypervisor layer, prevent unauthorized code execution. Trusted Platform Module (TPM) support and host attestation through vCenter ensure hosts boot into a known good configuration.

Virtual machine Secure Boot via UEFI provides guest operating system integrity verification, and VMware Tools packages are signed and verified.

vSphere Lifecycle Manager and Configuration Profiles maintain desired-state configuration for ESX hosts, with drift detection and automated remediation, identifying modifications from approved baselines.

For container workloads, Harbor registry integration offers image signing, vulnerability scanning, and content trust, preventing deployment of compromised images.

VMware vDefend directly addresses malicious code detection and prevention through its Malware Prevention Service, IDS/IPS, and network detection and response capabilities.

The vDefend Malware Prevention Service, deployed as a service virtual machine on ESX hosts, inspects files and data streams for malicious content in real time, detecting both known and unknown threats, including zero-day exploits, through signature-based detection and behavioral analysis. It classifies malware by family and assigns threat verdicts, providing dynamic analysis for detailed behavioral insights.

Beyond file-based detection, vDefend includes fileless malware detection, identifying script-based and in-memory attacks by collecting forensic evidence such as process IDs, command lines, and hashes.

The vDefend IDS/IPS provides signature-based and behavioral intrusion detection and prevention at the distributed firewall level, detecting network-borne malware delivery, command-and-control communications, and lateral movement. This operates at the virtual network interface level, offering visibility into east-west traffic.

The Malware Prevention Dashboard in Security Services Platform aggregates detection events, providing centralized visibility for identifying outbreaks and tracking trends.

While vDefend provides extensive capabilities for detecting and preventing malicious code from executing and spreading across the network, full eradication of malware from an already-infected guest operating system typically requires dedicated endpoint security tools operating within the guest. Organizations must implement these host-level remediation tools and establish incident response processes to fully clean infected systems.
